coredata.dc:description: The design of contrast agents (CAs) with high magnetic relaxivities is a key issue in the field of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The traditional strategy employed is aimed at optimizing the structural design of the magnetic atoms in the CA. However, it is difficult to obtain an agent with magnetic relaxivity over 100 mM−1 s−1 using this approach. In this work, we demonstrate that modulation of the localized superacid microenvironment of certain CAs (Gd3+ loaded polyethylene glycol modified graphene oxide quantum dots or ‘GPG’ for short) can effectively enhance the longitudinal magnetic relaxivities (r 1) by accelerating proton exchange. r 1 values of a series of GPGs are significantly increased by 20–30 folds compared to commercially available CAs over a wide range of static magnetic field strengths (e.g. 210.9 mM−1 s−1 vs. 12.3 mM−1 s−1 at 114 μT, 127.0 mM−1 s−1 vs. 4.9 mM−1 s−1 at 7.0 T). GPG aided MRI images is then acquired both in vitro and in vivo with low biotoxicities. Furthermore, folic-acid-modified GPG is demonstrated suitable for MRI-fluorescence dual-modal tumor targeting imaging in animals with more than 98.3% specific cellular uptake rate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the GP8G. (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution histogram, and (c) HR-TEM image of GP8G. (d) AFM topography image of GP8G on a 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate. Inset: height profile analysis along the line shown in the image. (e) C 1s and O 1s spectra of the GOQDs, GOQDs-PEG8, and GP8G. (f) Gd 4 d spectrum of GP8G. (g) Conductivity-titrant volume curve for GP8G. (h) MALDI-TOF-MASS spectra of the GOQDs, GOQDs-PEG8, and GP8G. (i) Illustration of the structure of GP8G: PEG chains are conjugated onto the surfaces of the GOQDs, and Gd3+ are coordinated with oxygen atoms in the PEG chains.

Magnetic properties and GQD modulation of GPG at ULF. (a) Linear fit to find the r 1 value of the GP8G. (b) 1/T 1 values for GP8G (Ⅰ), rGP8G (Ⅱ), N-GP8G (Ⅲ), NH2-GP8G (Ⅳ), and H2O (Ⅴ). The Gd3+ concentration in structures (Ⅰ)–(Ⅳ) is 0.08 mM. The T 1 value of H2O is 2463.0 ± 59.6 ms. (c) T 1 values of GP8G and Gd-EOB-DTPA in H2O/D2O solutions with different amounts of D2O: T 1 and T 1o are the longitudinal relaxation times with and without D2O. The Gd3+ concentrations in GP8G and Gd-EOB-DTPA are 0.005 and 0.125 mM, respectively. The linear fits were used to analyze the effect of D2O on the mixtures.

Magnetic properties and PEG modulation of the GPG. (a) Relaxivities of GP n G-1 species with different PEG chains at ULF. (b) ΔG values of the coordination process between Gd3+ and PEG chains of different lengths. (c) ΔG values of the coordination process between Gd3+ and protonated PEG chains of different lengths. (d) 1H NMR spectra of GP6G-1 after titration with different concentrations of H2O. (e) Relative intensities of the H+ signals (IR) for GP4G-1, GP6G-1, and GP8G-1 plotted as a function of H2O concentration.

Comparison of relaxivity values. (a) Relaxivities of GPG species with different PEG chain lengths plotted against the molar ratio of PEG to Gd, and with PEG:GOQDs = 3.33 mmol g−1. (b) Comparison of relaxivities of the different GPGs, commercial CAs [7,8], and other reported CAs [15–18,21–23,54–68]. (c) Photograph of samples ready for T 1 mapping measurement. (d) T 1 map of 0.02 mM GP6G-3, 0.03 mM GP6G-3, 0.29 mM Gd-EOB-DTPA, 0.45 mM Gd-EOB-DTPA, 0.059 mM MnCl2, and 0.093 mM MnCl2 in aqueous solution and ULF.

Biotoxicity and MRI by GP6G-3 with ultra-high magnetic relaxivity. (a) Relative viabilities of PANC-1 cells after being incubated for 48 h with 400 μg mL−1 GP6G-3 (visualized by staining with a LIVE/DEAD Cell Viability Kit from Thermo Fisher Scientific). (b) Cell viability of fibroblasts and PANC-1 cells treated for 48 h with different concentrations (0–400 μg mL−1) of GP6G-3. (c) Cell viability of fibroblasts and PANC-1 cells treated for different times (0–72 h) with 400 μg mL−1 GP6G-3. OD and OD0 are the viabilities of the treated and untreated cells, respectively. (d) Pathological analysis of various organs in mice injected with GP6G-3 via the tail vein (tissue slices obtained 24 h post-injection). An in vivo MRI of a nude mouse is shown in (e) pre-injection, and then (f) 30 min post-injection. The mouse was injected subcutaneously and imaged using a 7.0 T animal MRI scanner: the portion in the carmine circle is a tumor.

Biotoxicity, cell uptake, and tumor-targeting imaging using FA-GP6G-3 in vitro. (a) The relative viabilities of PANC-1 cells after being incubated for 48 h with FA-GP6G-3 of concentration 400 μg mL−1. (b) Cell viabilities of fibroblasts and PANC-1 cells treated for 48 h with different concentrations of FA-GP6G-3. (c) Cell viabilities of fibroblasts and PANC-1 cells treated for different times (concentration = 400 μg mL−1). OD and OD0 are the viabilities of treated and untreated cells, respectively. (d) Bright field image and (e) FA-GP6G-3 channel image of the co-cultured system of fibroblasts (blue circles) and PANC-1 cells (carmine circles) incubated with FA-GP6G-3 (100 μg mL−1) for 24 h. (f) Confocal images of cells treated with 400 μg mL−1 of FA-GP6G-3 for 48 h. The images show the morphology and distribution of the FA-GP6G-3 in the cytoplasm of the fibroblasts. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vivo Tumor-targeting imaging using FA-GP6G-3. The fluorescent images (a) and the averaged fluorescence intensities (b) comparison of isolated tumor and organs harvested from nude mice at 6 h after the injection of GP6G-3 and FA-GP6G-3 via the tail vein. (c) The average fluorescence intensities of the isolated tumor and organs harvested from nude mice at different times after injection of FA-GP6G-3. (d) and (e) are in vivo tumor-specific fluorescence images of nude mice taken pre-injection and 6 h after injection via the tail vein, respectively. The carmine circle highlights the tumor.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the magnetic relaxivity of MRI contrast agents via the localized superacid microenvironment of graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of contrast agents (CAs) with high magnetic relaxivities is a key issue in the field of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The traditional strategy employed is aimed at optimizing the structural design of the magnetic atoms in the CA. However, it is difficult to obtain an agent with magnetic relaxivity over 100 mM−1 s−1 using this approach. In this work, we demonstrate that modulation of the localized superacid microenvironment of certain CAs (Gd3+ loaded polyethylene glycol modified graphene oxide quantum dots or ‘GPG’ for short) can effectively enhance the longitudinal magnetic relaxivities (r 1) by accelerating proton exchange. r 1 values of a series of GPGs are significantly increased by 20–30 folds compared to commercially available CAs over a wide range of static magnetic field strengths (e.g. 210.9 mM−1 s−1 vs. 12.3 mM−1 s−1 at 114 μT, 127.0 mM−1 s−1 vs. 4.9 mM−1 s−1 at 7.0 T). GPG aided MRI images is then acquired both in vitro and in vivo with low biotoxicities. Furthermore, folic-acid-modified GPG is demonstrated suitable for MRI-fluorescence dual-modal tumor targeting imaging in animals with more than 98.3% specific cellular uptake rate.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has witnessed an enormous increase in application in the last few years and has emerged as one of the most powerful imaging techniques in diagnostic clinical medicine and biomedical research [1]. Contrast agents (CAs) are widely used in clinical MRI diagnosis applications (especially those involving kidney cancer [2] and prostate cancer [3], among others) as these agents enhance the contrast-to-noise ratio of the images and reduce the scanning time [4].

One group of commercially available Gadolinium (III)-based chelates, e.g. Magnevist® (Gd-DTPA), Dotarem® (Gd-DOTA) and Primovist® (Gd-EOB-DTPA), are image-brightening CAs that act by affecting the longitudinal relaxation time, T 1 [5,6]. However, these commercial Gd chelate compounds have low longitudinal relaxivity (r 1) values that range from 3.1 to 12.3 mM−1 s−1 over the magnetic field ranging from 127 μT to 7.0 T [7,8]. Having CAs with high r 1 values is highly advantageous as it effectively reduces the amount of toxic Gd3+ used in the clinical diagnosis and thus reduces the risk of patients suffering nephrogenic systemic fibrosis [9–11] and cerebral disease [12]. Meanwhile, higher r 1 values also increase the contrast in the images which is clinically important for earlier diagnosis of cancer and other diseases. Furthermore, agents with ultra-high relaxivity (over 100 mM−1 s−1) could be promising candidates for molecular imaging [13,14]. Thus, the development of novel high-r 1 CAs would have a significant impact on MRI.

A great deal of efforts have been made to synthesize such high-r 1 CAs [15–19]. The main strategy traditionally used in such attempts is to control the coordinate structure of the magnetic atoms (e.g. coordinating water to the Gd3+ ion and/or changing the coordination strength between the Gd3+ and other ligands) [15,20]. However, as it is limited by the coordination ability of the Gd3+ ion, the above approach fails to produce r 1 values over 100 mM−1 s−1 [21–23]. Meanwhile, controlling the coordinate structure also results in an increase in the residence time of the water molecules which has negative effect on r 1 enhancement [1]. To solve the above problems, Wilson et al. synthesized ultra-short single-walled carbon nanotubes containing Gd3+
n -ion clusters (‘gadonanotubes’) and thus managed to achieve an ultra-high r 1 value of 635 mM−1 s−1 at 235 μT [15]. The enhancement was found to mainly derive from the carboxylic acid groups at the ends of the carbon nanotubes and at the defects [24]. Unfortunately, the synthesis of the specific ultra-short carbon nanotubes (i.e. length less than 100 nm and a carboxylated surface, etc.) required harsh preparation methods by using fluorine and argon (at 1000 °C) to carry out oxidation [25]. Interestingly, an obvious increase in r 1 value with gadonanotubes could be obtained in acidic conditions (aqueous HCl, pH＜3) at 1.5 T [26], which was explained as being due to an increase in the number of exchangeable protons [4]. Of course, such an acidic environment has a limited applicability in the buffered systems in organisms that require stable physiological pH values. Nevertheless, this work has inspired us to reconsider the structural design of CAs with high r 1 values.

More specifically, we demonstrate here that the r 1 values of certain CAs can be effectively enhanced by speeding up the proton exchange rate via the modulation of a localized superacid microenvironment. Polyethylene glycol (PEG), as a class of water-soluble, non-toxic, and non-immunogenic polymer, is widely used in biopharmaceuticals [27,28]. The unique structure of PEG also makes it possible to be conjoiners between GQDs and Gd chelates to form CAs, which shows a relaxivity 16 times higher than those of commercial CAs [19]. Therefore, the CAs used here are based on Gd3+ loaded PEG modified graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). These Gd3+-PEG-GOQDs, which we abbreviate to GPG in this work, show high r 1 values corresponding to 210.9 mM−1 s−1 at a magnetic field of 114 μT. This is almost 20 times higher than that of the commercially-available CA Gd-EOB-DTPA at the same magnetic field strength [7]. Meanwhile, GPG has an r 1 value of up to 127.0 mM−1 s−1 at 7.0 T compared to the r 1 of 4.9 mM−1 s−1 of Gd-EOB-DTPA. Moreover, the acidic environment used is not a macroscopic one but is localized (in the microenvironment around the GOQDs) [26]. This means the CA can be safely used in biological environments. In this work, we investigate its MRI performance both in vitro and in vivo with respect to the visible contrast enhancement of the images produced. In addition, we further find that we can modify the surfaces of the GOQDs (with folic acid) to enhance the CAs tumor-targeting ability producing a targeting rate of 98.3% in a co-culture system. Finally, tumor-targeting fluorescence imaging is also demonstrated both in vitro and in vivo.

The GOQDs, reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs, Fig. S1–S3), nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs, Fig. S1, S4, and S5), and amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (NH2-GQDs, Fig. S1, S6–S7) were purchased from Shanghai Xiwang Technology Co., Ltd. (China) and used as received. Ethylene glycol (PEG1, 99.8%), diethylene glycol (PEG2, 99.5%), triethylene glycol (PEG3, 99.0%), tetraethylene glycol (PEG4, 99.0%), pentaethylene glycol (PEG5, 98.0%), hexaethylene glycol (PEG6, 97.0%), octaethylene glycol (PEG8, 98.0%), gadolinium trinitrate (98%), and manganese chloride (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. Gadoxetate disodium (Gd-EOB-DTPA, 98%) was purchased from Regent Science Industry Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received. Deionized water (resistivity ~18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C) was obtained using a Milli-Q system and used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Gd3+ loaded PEG8 modified GOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GOQDs were synthesized by oxidizing graphene oxide via hydrothermal method [29,30], which generated abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of GOQDs. Typically, the Gd3+ loaded PEG8 modified GOQDs (GP8G) were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment. First, 0.2 mmol of PEG8 and 15 mg of GOQDs were added to 15 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then transferred to a para-polyphenol lined (PPL) autoclave and heated for 72 h at 240 °C. The product obtained was the GOQDs-PEG8 required. Next, 0.002 mmol of Gd(NO3)3 was added to the mixture which was then heated for another 72 h at 240 °C. The solution obtained was then dialyzed in a 3500-Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove any free Gd3+. The methods used to synthesize the different GPG structures (GP1G-1, GP2G-1, etc.) can be found in the Supporting Information (see Appendix).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of folic acid modified GP6G-3
ce:section.ce:para: The folic acid (FA) contains both amido groups and carboxyl. Our previous work has demonstrated the feasibility of surface modification of graphene quantum dots by FA with amido bonds via hydrothermal treatment [31]. Moreover, the catalysis of localized superacid microenvironment on the form of amido bonds was also demonstrated [32]. Therefore, we synthesized FA modified GP6G-3 (FA-GP6G-3) with amido bonds via hydrothermal treatment. In this case, 1 mg of GP6G-3 and 0.1 mM of folic acid were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then transferred to the PPL autoclave and heated for 12 h at 120 °C. The solution thereby produced was then dialyzed in a 3500-Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove free folic acid.

The relaxation times (T 1) of the samples were predominantly measured using a home-made ultra-low field (ULF) MRI system. Compared with clinical MRI, ULF MRI has a number of advantages, e.g. low-cost, high contrast between benign and malignant tissues, and imaging in presence of metal without susceptibility artifacts [35–37]. The typical measurement magnetic field (B 0) of ULF MRI is below 250 μT. To enhance the signal-to-noise ratio, B 0 of 114 μT was chosen in our system because at this field strength the corresponding Larmor frequency of proton (~4854 Hz) located at a frequency range with relatively low environmental noise. A so-called pre-polarization field (B p) with amplitude typically more than 2 orders of magnitude stronger than that of B 0 was introduced to enhance the signal amplitude before carrying out the standard imaging sequence [38]. Ultra-sensitive superconducting quantum interference devices (SQUIDs) with gradiometer configuration installed in a liquid helium cryostat were used to detect signals [39]. Various environmental noise-suppression techniques were applied to further improve the signal-to-noise ratio [40,41].

In this paper, the samples investigated using the ULF MRI system were sealed into 25 mL sample vials and placed beneath the cryostat during the measurement process. All relaxometry measurements were performed using this system, unless stated otherwise. The pulse sequences used for T 1 measurement and T 1 mapping at ULF are shown in Fig. S14 and S15, respectively. In both sequences, the samples (or phantom) were first pre-polarized by the B p field for a period of T p = 500 ms. After the B p field was switched-off adiabatically, the magnetization of the sample freely relaxed in the B 0 field for an evolution time
Δ
T 1
d e l a y
. Then, π/2 and π pulses were applied to allow the spin echo signals to be acquired. A series of
Δ
T 1
d e l a y
values were chosen to derive the T 1 values for each region of interest based on single-exponential decay fits of signal amplitude vs.
Δ
T 1
d e l a y
. For T 1 mapping purposes, pulsed phase-encoding gradients G
x (−45 to 90 μT/m) were applied for T pe = 32 ms in 16 steps. The T 1 map was obtained by calculating the T 1 values of each pixel in the 490 × 410 image. The acquisition time T a was 80 ms.

Further high-field measurements of r 1 were conducted using 0.5 T (Suzhou Niumag Analytical Instrument Corporation, China) and 7.0 T MRI scanners (Bruker Biospec 70/20 USR, Germany). Samples were prepared by drawing GPG samples with different Gd3+ concentrations into 0.5 mL centrifuge tubes.

The geometries of the PEG1-Gd to PEG8-Gd species and their proton complexes were optimized at the B3LYP/6–311++G** level and their energies calculated using the Jaguar program [42]. To obtain free energies, we diagonalized the mass-reduced Hessian to obtain the vibrational frequencies, which were then used to evaluate their thermodynamic properties at 298.15 K and 1 atm. The solvation effects in water were then simulated using the standard Poisson–Boltzmann solvation model [43,44] at the B3LYP/6–311++G** level based on the gas-phase optimized geometries.

In vivo MRI experiments were carried out using a 7.0 T animal MRI scanner (Bruker Biospec 70/20 USR). Isoflurane was used to anaesthetize the mice which were then laid face down on the sample plane for scanning. T 1-weighted MRI was then performed in the coronal plane using a multi-slice spin-echo sequence with the following parameters: TE = 6.5 ms, TR = 1500 ms, averages = 2, repetitions = 1, echo spacing = 6.5 ms, rare factor = 4, image size = 256 × 256, field of view = 30 mm × 30 mm, slice thickness = 1 mm, slices = 20.
All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Shanghai Ninth People's Hospital.

TEM images of the Gd3+ loaded PEG8 modified GOQDs (GP8G) and a histogram of the corresponding size distribution are shown in Fig. 1a–1c . The GP8G can be seen to be homogeneous with a lateral size distribution of 2.0–7.0 nm and an average diameter of 4.0 nm. The average diameter of the GOQDs is 3.9 nm, as shown in Fig. S16 and S17. No obvious change in lateral size can be observed after surface modification of the GOQDs.

Fig. 1d displays a representative AFM image of highly-dispersed GP8G on a 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate. The thicknesses of the GP8Gs are approximately 0.5–1.5 nm, which means that they have structures consisting of 1–4 layers [45]. The thickness underwent no obvious change after surface modification of the GOQDs with PEG (Fig. S18 and S19). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of an individual GP8G dot shown in Fig. 1c shows distinct signs of a crystal lattice with a spacing parameter of 0.24 nm which represents the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [46]. An AFM image of highly dispersed GOQDs on a 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate is shown in Fig. S18. The thickness of the GOQDs is approximately 0.5–1.5 nm, providing evidence for 1–4 layered structures. No obvious change in the thickness can be observed after surface modification (Fig. 1d and S19).

Raman spectra of the GOQDs and GP8G are given in Fig. S20. Intense defect-related D- and G-peaks can be observed in both the GOQDs and GP8G spectra at 1350 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the peak intensity ratios of these peaks (I D/I G) are 0.87 and 0.98 in the GOQDs and GP8G spectra, respectively. This increase in I D/I G ratio is indicative of the successful introduction of PEG and Gd3+.

The chemical structure of GP8G was further characterized using XPS, matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass (MALDI-TOF-MASS) spectrometry, and FT-IR spectrometry. XPS survey spectra of the GOQDs, PEG8 modified GOQDs (GOQDs-PEG8), and GP8G all show a C 1s peak at approximately 284 eV, along with an O 1s peak at approximately 532 eV (Fig. S21–S23). The oxygen content of the GOQDs and GP8G corresponds to 28 and 36 at.%, respectively [47]. The increased oxygen content of the GP8G can be attributed to the introduction of the PEG8 chains [48]. Moreover, obvious peaks at approximately 143 and 148 eV can be observed for GP8G due to the introduction of Gd3+. The Gd3+ content of GP8G is 2.5 at.% [49]. The C 1s and O 1s spectra of the GOQDs, GOQDs-PEG8, and GP8G are plotted in Fig. 1e. The peaks located at 284.5, 285.5, and 288.2 eV can be attributed to C–C/CC, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively, while the peaks located at 531.1 and 535.5 eV can be attributed to the CO and C–O/C–O–C bonds, respectively [49]. It is obvious that the introduction of PEG8 chains increases the C–O/C–O–C content in GOQDs-PEG8 and GP8G. Moreover, the Gd 4 d spectrum of GP8G (Fig. 1f) further indicates the presence of Gd3+ in GP8G. The conductivity–titrant volume curve for GP8G is shown in Fig. 1g. The relative ratio of C–OH to –COOH in GP8G is about 1.6:1. Consistent with the XPS results, the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S24) demonstrated that there is approximately one acidic site for every 3–4 carbon atoms. The large number of acidic sites results in the localized superacid environment in the GOQDs [32,50].

Fig. 1h shows the MALDI-TOF-MASS spectra of GOQDs, GOQDs-PEG8, and GP8G. The MALDI-TOF-MASS spectrum of GOQDs exhibits peaks with M/z values of 2300–3500, which indicates there are 120–180 aromatic nuclei in a single GOQD and matches the TEM results [51]. The mass distribution pattern in GOQDs-PEG8 shifts to higher masses by amounts equal to 352.4, 704.9, and 1057.3 M/z, which can be attributed to the additional attachment of 1–3 PEG8 chains via the formation of C–O–C bonds. Moreover, the mass distribution pattern from the GP8G shifts to even higher masses by amounts equal to 157.3 and 314.5 M/z compared to the GOQDs-PEG8 due to the additional attachment of 1–2 Gd3+ ions by the coordination of the Gd3+ ions to the PEG8. Therefore, the GP8G has the structure illustrated in Fig. 1i. Each GOQD particle is modified by 1–3 PEG8 chains. The Gd3+ combines with the PEG8 chains via the formation of coordinate bonds between the Gd3+ and PEG8. Typically, the Gd3+ content is 1–2 ions in every GOQD particle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic properties and localized superacid microenvironment of the GPG
ce:section.ce:para: The T 1 values of the samples were measured at 114 μT using the home-made ULF MRI system (unless otherwise stated). The T 1 values of the GP8G were found to be 244.6 ± 11.7, 375.3 ± 12.5, 914.4 ± 76.1, and 1072.7 ± 53.5 ms, when the concentrations of the Gd3+ were 0.08, 0.04, 0.016, and 0.008 mM, respectively (Fig. S25). The relaxivity was obtained by making a linear fit of the relaxation rate (1/T 1) data plotted as a function of the Gd3+ concentration. The r 1 value of GP8G was thus found to be 48.5 ± 5.7 mM−1 s−1 (Fig. 2 a). This is 4 times of the values obtained from commercial Gd-based CAs (for example, r 1-Gd-DTPA = 10.4 ± 0.2 mM−1 s−1 and r 1-Gd-EOB-DTPA = 12.3 ± 0.6 mM−1 s−1) [7]. It is also higher than that of Gd(NO3)3 (41.7 ± 2.8 mM−1 s−1, Fig. S26 and S27) measured under the same conditions.

The key role played by the localized superacid environment in enhancing the magnetic relaxivity can be proved by synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with different structures (rGQDs [49], N-GQDs [52], and NH2-GQDs [53]) following the methods described in the Experimental Section 1.3 of the Supporting Information. More detailed information is given in Table S1. As shown in Fig. 2b, the 1/T 1 value increases significantly as the concentration of the basic group content of the GQDs decreased (alkalinity of groups: NH2 > lattice doped > –OH > –COOH). The 1/T 1 values are 0.4, 0.6, 0.7, and 4.1 s−1 for NH2-GQDs, N-GQDs, rGQDs, and GOQDs, respectively (Fig. S28–S30). Note that the smallest 1/T 1 value is similar to that of H2O (Fig. S31).

The enhancement of the magnetic relaxivity via the localized superacid environment can also be proved via control experiments using mixtures of H2O and D2O (thus producing different D2O concentrations). It is known that the dissociation constant of D2O (pKa = 1.6 × 10−15) is much smaller than that of H2O (pKa = 10−14). Therefore, the localized acidity can be reduced by increasing the D2O concentration. Fig. 2c shows plots of the T 1/T 1o values of the GP8G in the H2O/D2O mixture vs. the D2O concentration (T 1o is the longitudinal relaxation time without D2O). The T 1 values of the GP8G (with Gd3+ concentration of 0.005 mM) are 1364.5 ± 47.5, 1507.5 ± 21.2, 1645.6 ± 66.6, and 1728.9 ± 89.9 ms when the D2O concentrations are 0, 25, 50, and 75 wt.%, respectively (Fig. S32). The linear relationship between T 1/T 1o and the concentration of D2O can be written in the form:
T 1
T
1 o
= k ×
C
D 2
O
+ 1 ,
where
C
D 2
O
is the concentration of the D2O in units of wt.% and k is a fitting coefficient which is found to be equal to 0.38 (wt.%)−1 for GP8G. In contrast, the T 1/T 1o values of a commercially available CA, Gd-EOB-DTPA (with a Gd3+ concentration of 0.125 mM) which is also shown in Fig. 2c, exhibit no obvious changes as the proportion of D2O in the mixture increases. In fact, the fitting procedure yields k = 0.02 (wt.%)−1, in this case. The T 1 values of the Gd-EOB-DTPA are 660.5 ± 17.0, 657.1 ± 21.4, 664.1 ± 48.0, and 677.3 ± 50.1 ms when the D2O concentrations are 0, 25, 50, and 75 wt.%, respectively, as shown in Fig. S33.

All of results above demonstrate the key role played by the localized superacid environment in the GOQDs in enhancing the magnetic relaxivity r 1 which can be attributed to the fast proton exchange process associated with the localized superacid environment.

The main change in r 1 value of GPG can be predicted using Solomon, Bloembergen and Morgan (SBM) theory [1,2]. The disposition of H2O molecules around the Gd3+ ion can be classified into two categories. One is the ‘inner-sphere’, in which the oxygen atom of the H2O is directly bonded to the Gd3+ while its H+ ions are exchanged with the bulk water protons in, for example, the solvent. The other one is the ‘outer-sphere’, in which the H2O hydrate in the complex is governed solely by translational diffusion and the distance of closest approach to the Gd3+ ion. The relaxivity arising from the inner-sphere water(s) is given by:
r 1 IS
=
q /
C
H 2
O
(
T
1 m
+
τ m
)
,
where
r 1 IS
is the relaxivity of the inner-sphere water, q represents the hydration number (number of fast-exchanging water molecules within the inner-sphere),
C
H 2
O
is the concentration of the water,
T
1 m
is the T 1 value of the water hydrogen in the inner-sphere, and
τ m
is the residence lifetime of a water proton in an inner-sphere water molecule. The localized superacid environment significantly increases the number of fast-exchanging protons in the inner-sphere (q), and hence the relaxivity r 1.

The effect of PEG concentration on relaxivity enhancement was first studied by preparing three structures with different PEG8 adding amounts of 0.2, 0.1, and 0.05 mmol, named as GP8-aG-1, GP8-bG-1, and GP8-cG-1 (also named as GP8G-1), respectively. The molar ratio of PEG to Gd was kept to be 1:1 (see Experimental Section 1.4 in the Supporting Information). As shown in Table S2 and Fig. S34–S37, r 1 of GP8-x G-1 (x = a, b, c) increased with the decrease of PEG amount in the structure, which was mainly due to the increasing coordinated water of Gd3+ ions that originated from the longer distance among Gd3+ ions according to the SBM theory. Thus, the PEG adding amount of 0.05 mmol was the basis throughout the following experiments.

Gd3+ loaded GOQDs modified using PEGs with different chain lengths (GP n G-1) were then synthesized to investigate the effect of PEG chain length on the localized superacid environment of the GOQDs (see Experimental Section 1.4 in the Supporting Information). Seven types of PEG were used: PEG1 (n = 1), PEG2 (n = 2), PEG3 (n = 3), PEG4 (n = 4), PEG5 (n = 5), PEG6 (n = 6), PEG8 (n = 8), as shown Table S2. The dosage of the modifiers (PEGs) and Gd3+ in each GOQD is 3.33 mmol g−1. The r 1 values of GP n G-1 are 1.5 ± 0.2, 1.7 ± 0.04, 1.2 ± 0.1, 3.6 ± 1.1, 1.6 ± 0.2, 2.4 ± 0.1, and 1.3 ± 0.1 mM−1 s−1, when the PEGs used are PEG1, PEG2, PEG3, PEG4, PEG5, PEG6, and PEG8, respectively (Fig. 3a and S34, S37–S49). GP4G-1 thus shows the highest r 1 value (3.6 ± 1.1 mM−1 s−1).

The thermodynamic properties of the coordinated Gd3+ compounds were derived using density functional theory (DFT). Fig. 3b and Table S3 shows the calculated changes in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) associated with the coordination processes occurring between the Gd3+ ion and different PEG chains (PEG1–PEG8). A lower ΔG value indicates greater thermodynamic stability. The ΔG value decreases significantly as the PEG chain length increases, which reflects the increased thermodynamic stability of the PEG-Gd bonds with longer PEG chains. When the PEG chains are PEG1–2, PEG3–4, and PEG5–8, the stable structures in the PEG-Gd complex are two-, tetra-, and hexa-coordinated, respectively. It is worth noting that the thermodynamic stabilities of PEG-Gd species with even numbered lengths (PEG2, PEG4, PEG6, and PEG8) are higher than those of their neighboring odd numbered lengths (PEG1, PEG3, PEG5, and PEG7).

On the other hand, the coordination with Gd3+ is helpful for the shielding of the reaction field. Indeed, as the stability of the PEG-Gd increases, the ΔG associated with the protonation of PEG1–6-Gd increases. The ΔG value reaches a maximum when the GOQDs are modified using PEG6 (Fig. 3c). The ΔG values of protonated PEG7-Gd and PEG8-Gd are lower due to the increasingly richer oxygen content of the longer PEG chains.

The proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) titration results (Fig. 3d and e) confirm the results of the DFT calculations. Fig. 3d shows the 1H NMR spectra of GP6G-1 with different concentrations of H2O. The broad peaks located at 5.4 ppm can be attributed to the H+ ions on protonated PEG chains. Increasing the H2O concentration increases the protonation of the PEG chains which means the PEG chains will have a greater polarizing effect on the inner-sphere. Fig. 3e shows plots of the relative intensity (
I R
) of the H+ signals in GP4G-1, GP6G-1, and GP8G-1 as a function of H2O concentration calculated using:
I R
=
H p
H PEG
/ L
,
where
H p
is the integrated intensity of the H+ signal from the protonated PEG chains,
H PEG
is the integrated intensity of the CH2 signal from the protonated PEG chains, and L is the number of H atoms in the PEG chain. As can be seen, GP4G-1 yields the largest
I R
values for a given H2O concentration as there is a strong polarizing effect from the PEG4 chains on the H2O in the inner-sphere. In contrast, GP6G-1 produces the smallest
I R
values which can be attributed to the presence of a weak polarization effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gd3+ content reduction for higher relaxivity
ce:section.ce:para: The Gd3+ content in the GPG system can be further adjusted to obtain even higher r 1 values. Three GP4G structures with the same PEG4 concentration (3.33 mmol g−1) but different molar ratios between the PEG and Gd3+ (GP4G-1, PEG4 : Gd = 1; GP4G-2, PEG4 : Gd = 10; GP4G-3, PEG4 : Gd = 100) were prepared (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 4 a, decreasing the Gd3+ content significantly enhances r 1 from 3.6 ± 1.1 to 69.8 ± 2.9 mM−1 s−1 (Fig. S44 and S45, S50–S52, and S55). The results thus indicate that higher r 1 values can be obtained by using less Gd3+.

We then prepared two other samples, GP6G-3 and GP8G-3, with the same PEG : Gd ratio of 100:1 (see Table S2). As shown in Fig. 4a (and Fig. S53–S55 in the Supporting Information), the relaxivities of the GP6G-3 and GP8G-3 species are increased to 210.9 ± 17.2 and 116.1 ± 15.4 mM−1 s−1, respectively. The experimental measurements using an ULF coincide with the DFT calculation results. It is worth noting that this r 1 value for GP6G-3 is 20 times larger than that of the commercial CAs at the same magnetic field strength (114 μT) [7].

The main change in r 1 value of GPG can be explained by SBM theory. The decrease of Gd3+ amount in GPG structure leads to the isolation of Gd3+ ions. An individual Gd3+ ion could thus have more chance to coordinate with the oxygen atom of H2O. With the abundant coordinated H2O molecules, the exchange rate of protons among coordinated H2O and bulk H2O molecules could be thus accelerated, which causes the increase of q and results in the enhancement of r 1 in GPG structure.

It is interesting to note that GP4G-1 show higher r 1 in the group of GP n G-1, while the GP6G-3 exhibit highest r 1 among GPnG-3. As shown in Fig. 3c, the thermodynamic calculations exhibit that the thermodynamic stabilities of the coordinated structures of protonated PEG4 and PEG6 are higher than the others. Thus, both GP4G-1 and GP6G-1 display a high r 1 among the group of GP n G-1 (Fig. 3a). When the molar ratio of PEG to Gd is 1:1, PEGs are fully coordinated with Gd3+ ions. Because of the shorter chain length of PEG4, the strong steric hindrance effect [69,70] on the surface of GP4G-1 brings faster exchange rate of protons than that on the surface of GP6G-1. Therefore, GP4G-1 has larger r 1 compared to GP6G-1. With the higher molar ratio of PEG to Gd (such as 100:1), the steric hindrance effect gets weaker, and the thermodynamic stability dominates the r 1 enhancement of GP n G-3. As a comparison, the r 1 of GP6G-3 is 3 times higher than that of GP4G-3 when the molar ratio of PEG to Gd is 100:1 (Fig. S55).

In contrast, the relaxation time T 1 of PEG6-Gd-3 without GOQDs (see Fig. S56) reaches 1393.8 ± 141.0 ms when the Gd3+ concentration is 0.05 mM. Such a long relaxation time indicates that the introduction of the GOQDs can efficiently speed up the exchange rate of the protons, thus leading to large r 1 values. Moreover, GP6G-3 yields very high r 1 values of 210.9, 84.9, and 53.4 mM−1 s−1 using fields of 114 μT, 0.5 T, and 7.0 T, respectively (Fig. S55, S57, and S58). These results are much higher than those of commercial CAs and most Gd-based CAs previously reported (Tables S4 and S5, Fig. 4b) at ULF (＜5 × 10−4 T), low field (LF, 5 × 10−4 to 1 T), and high field (HF, ＞1 T).

The difference between the magnetic relaxivities of GP6G-3 and a commercial CA (Gd-EOB-DTPA) is illustrated more explicitly by the T 1 map shown in Fig. 4d. The T 1 values of 0.02 mM GP6G-3, 0.03 mM GP6G-3, 0.29 mM Gd-EOB-DTPA, 0.45 mM Gd-EOB-DTPA, 0.059 mM MnCl2, and 0.093 mM MnCl2 are 333, 206, 315, 205, 268, and 164 ms, respectively. Thus, the 0.03 mM GP6G-3 aqueous solution has essentially the same T 1 value as the 0.45 mM Gd-EOB-DTPA aqueous solution but, crucially, the Gd3+ concentration in the GP6G-3 solution is 15 times lower than that in the other. A similar situation can be found with the 0.02 mM GP6G-3 solution and 0.29 mM Gd-EOB-DTPA solution. The much lower concentration of Gd3+ in GP6G-3 significantly reduces the potential risk of toxic side effects occurring. This suggests that GP6G-3 may prove to be of great value in clinical applications. The T 1 values of the MnCl2 aqueous solutions of different concentration yields an r 1 value of 69.6 mM−1 s−1 (Fig. S59), which is in good agreement with the reported values derived using a similar field strength [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity and MRI by GP6G-3 in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
The free Gd3+ ion concentration (
[
Gd
3 +
]
) and the total Gd concentration (
C
Gd
3 +
) of GPGs and commercial Gd based MRI contrast agents (characterized with ICP-OES) are shown in Table S6. The stability of Gd in GPGs and commercial MRI contrast agents could be evaluated by dissociation rate (Dr) given in Table S6. The Dr was calculated as follows:
D r
=
Gd
3 +
C
Gd
3 +
× 100 % .

The Dr of GPGs was 0.019–0.092%, which was significantly lower than those of commercial MRI contrast agents (0.54–0.71%). Thus, the Gd3+ ions in the GPGs structure were much more stable compared to those in the commercial contrast agents. Moreover, the ultra-high relaxivity of GPG allowed the dramatic reduction of Gd3+ ion uptake amount in clinical diagnosis while obtaining similar T 1 contrast, which remarkably decreased the possibility of suffering nephrogenic systemic fibrosis.

The bio-compatibility of the GP6G-3 was first investigated by probing the metabolic activity of fibroblast and PANC-1 cells treated with GP6G-3 (400 μg mL−1) for 48 h (Fig. 5 a). No significant reduction in cell viability was observed. The bio-compatibility of the GP6G-3 was also investigated by probing the metabolic activity of fibroblast and PANC-1 cells treated with different concentrations of GP6G-3 (0–400 μg mL−1) for 48 h, as shown in Fig. 5b. The cells were cultured in 96-well plates with various concentrations of the GP6G-3, and then incubated for 48 h. Subsequently, a standard assay was performed to assess the cell viabilities. No significant reduction in cell viability was observed even in the cells treated with the highest concentration of GP6G-3 used (400 μg mL−1). These results indicate that the GP6G-3 prepared by our proposed method is bio-compatible and nontoxic in vitro. Moreover, the fibroblast and PANC-1 cells showed no significant reduction in cell viability even after a long treatment time (72 h) when 400 μg mL−1 GP6G-3 was used (Fig. 5c).

The in vivo toxicity of GP6G-3 was also evaluated by analyzing tissue slices from mice administered with GP6G-3 (Fig. 5d). No obvious inflammation, cell necrosis, or apoptosis was observed in the heart, kidney, lung, liver, and spleen tissues, which implies the complete absence of obvious side effects.

As GP6G-3 has a very high relaxivity value and good bio-compatibility, its potential application in MRI was further explored. The MRI performance of the GP6G-3 was then explored using a 7.0 T MRI scanner (Bruker Biospec 70/20 USR). T 1-weighted images of a region of clinical interest (a tumor) were acquired pre-injection and then 30 min later after injection of GP6G-3 at a dose of 2 mg kg−1 (Fig. 5e and f). The T 1-weighted image of the tumor clearly becomes brighter after the injection of the GP6G-3. The tumor and healthy tissue could thus be more readily distinguished. These results clearly demonstrate that PEG6G-3 can efficiently enhance the contrast of such images. This, of course, is a major factor of importance in the early diagnosis of tumor lesions.

Finally, we investigated the tumor-targeting ability of the GP6G-3 after surface modification with folic acid (i.e. FA-GP6G-3), which exhibits yellow photoluminescent (PL) properties (Fig. S60 and S61), high stability (Fig. S62), and outstanding anti-jamming capability (Fig. S63–S66).

Fig. 6 shows the results of tumor-specific fluorescence imaging experiments carried out in vitro and in vivo. The bio-compatibility of FA-GP6G-3 was first investigated by probing the metabolic activity of fibroblast and PANC-1 cells treated with different concentrations (0–400 μg mL−1) of FA-GP6G-3 for 48 h (Fig. 6a and b). Fig. 6a shows the relative viabilities of PANC-1 cells treated with FA-GP6G-3 of concentration 400 μg mL−1 for 48 h. To generate the data shown in Fig. 6b, cells were cultured in 96-well plates with various concentrations of FA-GP6G-3, and then incubated for 48 h. No significant reduction in cell viability was observed for the cells even when the highest concentration of FA-GP6G-3 (400 μg mL−1) was used. Thus, the FA-GP6G-3 prepared using our proposed method is bio-compatible and nontoxic in vitro. Moreover, cells showed no significant reduction in cell viability after prolonged treatment time (72 h using FA-GP6G-3 of concentration 400 μg mL−1) as can be seen from Fig. 6c. PANC-1 cells and fibroblasts were then co-cultured and incubated with FA-GP6G-3 (100 μg mL−1) for 24 h [31]. Fig. 6d shows the fibroblasts and PANC-1 cells imaged in a bright field (marked with blue and carmine circles, respectively). Fig. 6e shows that the PANC-1 cells emit 550 nm yellow PL in the GP6G-3 channel. The cells marked by carmine circles are consistent in the FA-GP6G-3 channel and overlap, which demonstrates that only the PANC-1 cells took the FA-GP6G-3 in and then emitted yellow PL. In the co-cultured group of PANC-1 cells and fibroblasts, the ratios of the cells that were labeled by the GP6G-3 without folic acid modification to all cells were 62.3% (PANC-1) and 51.7% (fibroblast). In contrast, the ratios of the cells that were labeled by the FA-GP6G-3 to all cells were 98.3% (PANC-1) and 6.84% (fibroblast). Thus, the FA-GP6G-3 have good selectivity towards tumor cells.

Fig. 6f shows confocal images of PANC-1 cells stained with FA-GP6G-3 (yellow), phalloidin (red), and 4’,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (blue). The stains allow the cytoskeleton (red) and nuclei (blue) to be clearly marked and the granular FA-GP6G-3-stained parts can be clearly observed in the cytoplasm with clear zones between the cell membrane and nuclei.

Based on the in vitro results in Fig. 6, the in vivo fluorescence-imaging experiments were performed to evaluate the tumor-targeting ability of the FA-GP6G-3 in a nude mouse bearing a PANC-1 tumor. The tumor-bearing nude mice were i.v. injected with GP6G-3 and FA-GP6G-3 (150 μL; 5 mg kg−1), respectively. The distributions of GP6G-3 and FA-GP6G-3 were recorded via PL imaging. As can be seen from Fig. 7a and 7b , strong fluorescence was emitted from the isolated tumor site 6 h after the i.v. injection of the FA-GP6G-3. However, the tumor harvested from the mouse showed weak fluorescence 6 h after the i.v. injection of the GP6G-3, and most of the GP6G-3 were transferred to liver and kidney. The time dynamics of the tumor-targeting behavior was studied by collecting tumors and organs from the nude mice at different times (0–6 h) after the injection of FA-GP6G-3 (Fig. 7c). The fluorescence intensity of the tumor site became stronger with the increase of incubation time, and the other organs kept emitting fluorescence at a low level. The in vivo tumor-specific fluorescence images of nude mice are depicted in Fig. 7d and e, which showed that the tumor in nude mouse emitted strong fluorescence 6 h after the i.v. injection with FA-GP6G-3 compared with the pre-injection one. This clearly demonstrates that the FA-GP6G-3 has an excellent ability to target tumors and be retained therein.

In summary, we have demonstrated that the magnetic relaxivity of CAs can be effectively enhanced by modulating the localized superacid microenvironment introduced by GOQDs. The superacidic microenvironment allows rapid proton exchange to occur. One of our novel CAs, GP6G-3, produced a remarkable magnetic relaxivity of 210.9 mM−1 s−1 at 114 μT, which is 20 times larger than those of commercial CAs at the same field strength. Moreover, the localized superacid microenvironment of the GOQDs ensures that the novel CAs are safe to use in biological environments.

The photoluminescent nature, low biotoxicity, and modifiable structure of the GOQDs mean that the new CAs can be used in MRI-fluorescence dual-modal applications (specific uptake rates of >98.3% were found in tumor cells). The GPGs should be cheap to produce on a large scale in the near future. In addition, their performance with respect to relaxivity can obviously be further improved. Thus, our work not only provides a new idea for designing novel multi-functional CAs with high magnetic relaxivities, but also opens up a new route to potentially important CAs for commercial use and exploitation.
